sections.title: Access & Flow Control — Insufficient Flow Control (CICD-SEC-1)

items.title: Direct pushes to protected branches
items.description: When the default/release branch accepts direct pushes, an attacker (or compromised contributor) can ship code straight to the branch that builds production, bypassing review entirely.
items.how_to_find: Access & Flow Control — Insufficient Flow Control (CICD-SEC-1): Access & Flow Control — Insufficient Flow Control (CICD-SEC-1): Access & Flow Control — Insufficient Flow Control (CICD-SEC-1): Access & Flow Control — Insufficient Flow Control (CICD-SEC-1): Access & Flow Control — Insufficient Flow Control (CICD-SEC-1): Confirm the default and release branches block direct pushes and require a pull/merge request
items.how_to_fix: Assign an owner for direct pushes to protected branches, apply vendor or CIS hardening guidance, validate in staging, then re-run the same test that exposed the gap.

items.title: Auto-merge enabled without guardrails
items.description: Auto-merge that completes a PR as soon as status checks pass can merge unreviewed changes if required human approval is not also enforced, collapsing the review gate.
items.how_to_find: Access & Flow Control — Insufficient Flow Control (CICD-SEC-1): Access & Flow Control — Insufficient Flow Control (CICD-SEC-1): Access & Flow Control — Insufficient Flow Control (CICD-SEC-1): Access & Flow Control — Insufficient Flow Control (CICD-SEC-1): Access & Flow Control — Insufficient Flow Control (CICD-SEC-1): Check that auto-merge requires the same approvals/reviews as a manual merge, and that bot-authored or dependency-bump PRs cannot auto-merge into protected branches without a human reviewer
items.how_to_fix: Assign an owner for auto-merge enabled without guardrails, apply vendor or CIS hardening guidance, validate in staging, then re-run the same test that exposed the gap.

items.title: Self-approval of changes
items.description: If the change author can also approve their own pull/merge request, the review control is illusory — a single compromised account can both author and approve malicious code.
items.how_to_find: Access & Flow Control — Insufficient Flow Control (CICD-SEC-1): Access & Flow Control — Insufficient Flow Control (CICD-SEC-1): Access & Flow Control — Insufficient Flow Control (CICD-SEC-1): Access & Flow Control — Insufficient Flow Control (CICD-SEC-1): Access & Flow Control — Insufficient Flow Control (CICD-SEC-1): Verify branch protection prevents the author from satisfying the required-reviewer count, and that dismissed/stale approvals are re-requested after new commits
items.how_to_fix: Assign an owner for self-approval of changes, apply vendor or CIS hardening guidance, validate in staging, then re-run the same test that exposed the gap.

items.title: Unverified artifact promotion across environments
items.description: Promoting build artifacts from dev to staging to production without an enforced gate lets an artifact that never passed review or scanning reach production.
items.how_to_find: Access & Flow Control — Insufficient Flow Control (CICD-SEC-1): Access & Flow Control — Insufficient Flow Control (CICD-SEC-1): Access & Flow Control — Insufficient Flow Control (CICD-SEC-1): Access & Flow Control — Insufficient Flow Control (CICD-SEC-1): Access & Flow Control — Insufficient Flow Control (CICD-SEC-1): Confirm each environment promotion requires an explicit approval/gate and that the promoted artifact digest matches the one that passed earlier stages
items.how_to_fix: Assign an owner for unverified artifact promotion across environments, apply vendor or CIS hardening guidance, validate in staging, then re-run the same test that exposed the gap.

references.title: Palo Alto — What is CI/CD security?

items.title: Merge allowed on stale or skipped status checks
items.description: Allowing merges when required checks are stale, skipped, or not yet completed lets unvetted code merge despite a façade of CI enforcement.
items.how_to_find: Access & Flow Control — Insufficient Flow Control (CICD-SEC-1): Access & Flow Control — Insufficient Flow Control (CICD-SEC-1): Access & Flow Control — Insufficient Flow Control (CICD-SEC-1): Access & Flow Control — Insufficient Flow Control (CICD-SEC-1): Access & Flow Control — Insufficient Flow Control (CICD-SEC-1): Verify branches are required to be up to date before merge and that required checks cannot be marked skipped/neutral to satisfy the gate
items.how_to_fix: Assign an owner for merge allowed on stale or skipped status checks, apply vendor or CIS hardening guidance, validate in staging, then re-run the same test that exposed the gap.

sections.title: Access & Flow Control — Identity & Access Management (CICD-SEC-2)

items.title: Over-privileged service accounts
items.description: CI service accounts and deploy identities granted broad org/admin or cloud roles turn any pipeline compromise into a full environment takeover.
items.how_to_find: Access & Flow Control — Identity & Access Management (CICD-SEC-2): Access & Flow Control — Identity & Access Management (CICD-SEC-2): Access & Flow Control — Identity & Access Management (CICD-SEC-2): Access & Flow Control — Identity & Access Management (CICD-SEC-2): Access & Flow Control — Identity & Access Management (CICD-SEC-2): Enumerate the permissions of each CI/deploy identity and confirm they follow least privilege — scoped to the specific repos, registries, and cloud resources they actually use
items.how_to_fix: Assign an owner for over-privileged service accounts, apply vendor or CIS hardening guidance, validate in staging, then re-run the same test that exposed the gap.

references.title: Palo Alto — What is CI/CD security?

items.title: Stale and orphaned access
items.description: Accounts of departed staff, unused PATs, deploy keys, and former integrations that are never revoked become unmonitored entry points into the pipeline.
items.how_to_find: Access & Flow Control — Identity & Access Management (CICD-SEC-2): Access & Flow Control — Identity & Access Management (CICD-SEC-2): Access & Flow Control — Identity & Access Management (CICD-SEC-2): Access & Flow Control — Identity & Access Management (CICD-SEC-2): Access & Flow Control — Identity & Access Management (CICD-SEC-2): Review org members, outside collaborators, deploy keys, and tokens for last-used dates; confirm a deprovisioning process removes access on offboarding and that unused credentials are revoked
items.how_to_fix: Assign an owner for stale and orphaned access, apply vendor or CIS hardening guidance, validate in staging, then re-run the same test that exposed the gap.

items.title: Shared or non-attributable accounts
items.description: Shared bot/admin accounts and personal tokens used for automation break attribution, so malicious actions in the pipeline cannot be traced to an individual.
items.how_to_find: Access & Flow Control — Identity & Access Management (CICD-SEC-2): Access & Flow Control — Identity & Access Management (CICD-SEC-2): Access & Flow Control — Identity & Access Management (CICD-SEC-2): Access & Flow Control — Identity & Access Management (CICD-SEC-2): Access & Flow Control — Identity & Access Management (CICD-SEC-2): Confirm automation uses dedicated, attributable machine identities (apps/service accounts) rather than shared logins, and that MFA/SSO is enforced on human accounts
items.how_to_fix: Assign an owner for shared or non-attributable accounts, apply vendor or CIS hardening guidance, validate in staging, then re-run the same test that exposed the gap.

references.title: GitHub — Security hardening for GitHub Actions

items.title: Unrestricted self-registration and external joins
items.description: Open SSO/self-registration into the SCM org or CI tenant lets unintended users gain repository or pipeline access, expanding the trust boundary silently.

items.how_to_fix: Assign an owner for unrestricted self-registration and external joins, apply vendor or CIS hardening guidance, validate in staging, then re-run the same test that exposed the gap.

items.title: Incomplete branch protection coverage
items.description: Branch protection applied to only some branches, or with admin bypass enabled, leaves release-relevant branches modifiable outside the intended controls.

sections.title: Pipeline Execution Integrity — Poisoned Pipeline Execution (CICD-SEC-4)

items.title: Direct PPE via pipeline config changes
items.description: An attacker with write access to a branch that triggers CI edits the pipeline definition to run their own commands with the pipeline’s privileges and secrets.

items.how_to_fix: Assign an owner for indirect ppe via consumed build scripts, apply vendor or CIS hardening guidance, validate in staging, then re-run the same test that exposed the gap.

items.title: Build cache poisoning
items.description: Shared or unscoped build caches writable from untrusted branches/PRs can be poisoned, so a later trusted build restores attacker-controlled cached content.

items.how_to_fix: Assign an owner for build cache poisoning, apply vendor or CIS hardening guidance, validate in staging, then re-run the same test that exposed the gap.

sections.title: Pipeline Execution Integrity — Workflow Input Injection

sections.title: Pipeline Execution Integrity — Runner & Build Node Security (CICD-SEC-7)

items.title: Persistent runners leaking state between jobs
items.description: Persistent (non-ephemeral) runners retain filesystem, env, and credential state across jobs, so one job can harvest secrets or tamper with another job’s build.

items.description: Attaching self-hosted runners to public repos lets external PR authors run code on your infrastructure — a well-known path to runner takeover and lateral movement.

items.title: Runners with excessive network access
items.description: Build runners able to reach internal networks, cloud metadata endpoints, and production systems turn a poisoned build into an internal pivot.

items.title: Preference for short-lived credentials
items.description: Using OIDC/federated, short-lived credentials instead of stored long-lived secrets removes the standing secret an attacker could steal from the pipeline.
items.how_to_find: Secrets & Credentials — Credential Hygiene & Rotation: Secrets & Credentials — Credential Hygiene & Rotation: Secrets & Credentials — Credential Hygiene & Rotation: Secrets & Credentials — Credential Hygiene & Rotation: Secrets & Credentials — Credential Hygiene & Rotation: Confirm cloud/registry access uses short-lived federated tokens (OIDC) where supported, and that any remaining static credentials are justified and minimized
items.how_to_fix: Assign an owner for preference for short-lived credentials, apply vendor or CIS hardening guidance, validate in staging, then re-run the same test that exposed the gap.

references.title: GitHub — Security hardening with OpenID Connect

items.title: No revocation path for leaked credentials
items.description: Without secret scanning and a defined revoke-and-rotate response, a leaked credential discovered in code or logs remains live and exploitable.
items.how_to_find: Secrets & Credentials — Credential Hygiene & Rotation: Secrets & Credentials — Credential Hygiene & Rotation: Secrets & Credentials — Credential Hygiene & Rotation: Secrets & Credentials — Credential Hygiene & Rotation: Secrets & Credentials — Credential Hygiene & Rotation: Confirm push protection / secret scanning is enabled and that there is a documented, tested process to revoke and rotate a credential immediately on detection
items.how_to_fix: Assign an owner for no revocation path for leaked credentials, apply vendor or CIS hardening guidance, validate in staging, then re-run the same test that exposed the gap.

references.title: GitHub — Using secrets in GitHub Actions

items.title: Insufficient secret-scanning coverage
items.description: Secret scanning that does not cover all repos, history, and artifact build outputs leaves blind spots where leaked credentials go unnoticed.
items.how_to_find: Secrets & Credentials — Credential Hygiene & Rotation: Secrets & Credentials — Credential Hygiene & Rotation: Secrets & Credentials — Credential Hygiene & Rotation: Secrets & Credentials — Credential Hygiene & Rotation: Secrets & Credentials — Credential Hygiene & Rotation: Confirm secret scanning runs across all repositories and full history, and that pipeline artifacts/images are also scanned for embedded credentials
items.how_to_fix: Assign an owner for insufficient secret-scanning coverage, apply vendor or CIS hardening guidance, validate in staging, then re-run the same test that exposed the gap.

items.title: Dependency confusion / substitution
items.description: When internal package names are resolvable from public registries, an attacker can publish a higher-version public package that the build pulls instead of the internal one.
items.how_to_find: Dependencies & Supply Chain — Dependency-Chain Abuse (CICD-SEC-3): Dependencies & Supply Chain — Dependency-Chain Abuse (CICD-SEC-3): Dependencies & Supply Chain — Dependency-Chain Abuse (CICD-SEC-3): Dependencies & Supply Chain — Dependency-Chain Abuse (CICD-SEC-3): Dependencies & Supply Chain — Dependency-Chain Abuse (CICD-SEC-3): Confirm internal scopes/namespaces are reserved, that the build pins a private registry for internal packages, and that public fallback cannot satisfy internal names
items.how_to_fix: Assign an owner for dependency confusion / substitution, apply vendor or CIS hardening guidance, validate in staging, then re-run the same test that exposed the gap.

items.title: Typosquatted and malicious packages
items.description: Packages named to mimic popular libraries (or compromised maintainer accounts) introduce malicious code that executes during install or build.
items.how_to_find: Dependencies & Supply Chain — Dependency-Chain Abuse (CICD-SEC-3): Dependencies & Supply Chain — Dependency-Chain Abuse (CICD-SEC-3): Dependencies & Supply Chain — Dependency-Chain Abuse (CICD-SEC-3): Dependencies & Supply Chain — Dependency-Chain Abuse (CICD-SEC-3): Dependencies & Supply Chain — Dependency-Chain Abuse (CICD-SEC-3): Verify dependencies against an allowlist/known-good set, check install scripts of new packages, and confirm new/low-reputation packages are reviewed before adoption
items.how_to_fix: Assign an owner for typosquatted and malicious packages, apply vendor or CIS hardening guidance, validate in staging, then re-run the same test that exposed the gap.

items.title: Unpinned dependencies and missing lockfiles
items.description: Floating version ranges without a committed lockfile let a freshly published (possibly malicious) version be resolved on the next build without review.
items.how_to_find: Dependencies & Supply Chain — Dependency-Chain Abuse (CICD-SEC-3): Dependencies & Supply Chain — Dependency-Chain Abuse (CICD-SEC-3): Dependencies & Supply Chain — Dependency-Chain Abuse (CICD-SEC-3): Dependencies & Supply Chain — Dependency-Chain Abuse (CICD-SEC-3): Dependencies & Supply Chain — Dependency-Chain Abuse (CICD-SEC-3): Confirm a lockfile is committed and used in CI (frozen/clean install), and that builds do not silently upgrade to new versions outside the locked set
items.how_to_fix: Assign an owner for unpinned dependencies and missing lockfiles, apply vendor or CIS hardening guidance, validate in staging, then re-run the same test that exposed the gap.

items.title: Unmonitored transitive dependency risk
items.description: Most code in a build comes from transitive dependencies; without an SBOM and continuous scanning, a vulnerable or malicious transitive package goes unnoticed.
items.how_to_find: Dependencies & Supply Chain — Dependency-Chain Abuse (CICD-SEC-3): Dependencies & Supply Chain — Dependency-Chain Abuse (CICD-SEC-3): Dependencies & Supply Chain — Dependency-Chain Abuse (CICD-SEC-3): Dependencies & Supply Chain — Dependency-Chain Abuse (CICD-SEC-3): Dependencies & Supply Chain — Dependency-Chain Abuse (CICD-SEC-3): Confirm an SBOM is generated and that transitive dependencies are scanned for known vulnerabilities and policy violations on every build
items.how_to_fix: Assign an owner for unmonitored transitive dependency risk, apply vendor or CIS hardening guidance, validate in staging, then re-run the same test that exposed the gap.

sections.title: Dependencies & Supply Chain — Third-Party Services & Actions (CICD-SEC-8)

references.title: OWASP CICD-SEC-8 — Ungoverned Usage of 3rd Party Services

items.title: Untrusted action publishers and marketplace risk
items.description: Adopting actions from unknown or unverified publishers grants third parties code execution and secret access inside your pipeline.

items.how_to_fix: Assign an owner for untrusted action publishers and marketplace risk, apply vendor or CIS hardening guidance, validate in staging, then re-run the same test that exposed the gap.

references.title: OWASP CICD-SEC-8 — Ungoverned Usage of 3rd Party Services

items.title: Over-privileged third-party integrations
items.description: Installed apps and SaaS integrations granted broad org/repo and token scopes become a supply-chain entry point if the third party is breached.

items.how_to_fix: Assign an owner for over-privileged third-party integrations, apply vendor or CIS hardening guidance, validate in staging, then re-run the same test that exposed the gap.

references.title: OWASP CICD-SEC-8 — Ungoverned Usage of 3rd Party Services

items.title: Transitive (nested) action dependencies
items.description: Actions that themselves call other actions extend the trust chain; an unpinned nested action can be compromised even if the top-level one is pinned.

items.how_to_fix: Assign an owner for transitive (nested) action dependencies, apply vendor or CIS hardening guidance, validate in staging, then re-run the same test that exposed the gap.

references.title: OWASP CICD-SEC-9 — Improper Artifact Integrity Validation

items.title: Missing build provenance / attestation
items.description: Without provenance attestations (who built it, from which source and pipeline), consumers cannot verify an artifact’s origin, undermining SLSA assurances.

references.title: OWASP CICD-SEC-9 — Improper Artifact Integrity Validation

items.title: Weak artifact registry access and immutability
items.description: Registries that allow tag overwrites or broad push access let an attacker replace a published artifact with a malicious one under the same name.

items.title: Publicly exposed CI dashboards and APIs
items.description: CI servers, build dashboards, and orchestration APIs reachable from the internet expose build status, logs, and often unauthenticated control, inviting takeover.

items.how_to_fix: Assign an owner for publicly exposed ci dashboards and apis, apply vendor or CIS hardening guidance, validate in staging, then re-run the same test that exposed the gap.

references.title: Palo Alto — What is CI/CD security?

items.title: Default or weak credentials on CI systems
items.description: CI orchestrators and plugins left with default admin credentials or weak auth give an attacker direct control of the build and deploy pipeline.

items.how_to_fix: Assign an owner for default or weak credentials on ci systems, apply vendor or CIS hardening guidance, validate in staging, then re-run the same test that exposed the gap.

items.title: Outdated CI/CD platform and plugins
items.description: Unpatched CI servers and plugins (a recurring source of CVEs and RCE) let an attacker compromise the orchestrator and everything it deploys.

items.how_to_fix: Assign an owner for outdated ci/cd platform and plugins, apply vendor or CIS hardening guidance, validate in staging, then re-run the same test that exposed the gap.

items.title: Ungoverned and inconsistent platform settings
items.description: Without enforced org-wide policy, individual repos/projects drift to insecure settings (open runners, disabled protections), creating inconsistent and weak spots.

items.how_to_fix: Assign an owner for ungoverned and inconsistent platform settings, apply vendor or CIS hardening guidance, validate in staging, then re-run the same test that exposed the gap.

items.title: Insufficient audit logging coverage
items.description: Missing audit logs for permission changes, secret access, pipeline edits, and deployments mean malicious actions in the pipeline go undetected.

references.title: OWASP CICD-SEC-10 — Insufficient Logging and Visibility

items.title: No centralized log aggregation or retention
items.description: Logs kept only locally on CI systems can be wiped by an attacker and are unavailable for correlation; short retention destroys evidence before investigation.

items.how_to_fix: Assign an owner for no centralized log aggregation or retention, apply vendor or CIS hardening guidance, validate in staging, then re-run the same test that exposed the gap.

references.title: OWASP CICD-SEC-10 — Insufficient Logging and Visibility

items.title: No detection or alerting on pipeline anomalies
items.description: Even with logs, the absence of alerting on suspicious events (new admin, secret exfil patterns, off-hours deploys) means attacks are only found after impact.

items.title: No runtime visibility into build execution
items.description: Without visibility into what build jobs actually do (network egress, process execution), poisoned pipeline execution and data exfiltration leave no observable trace.
